about.title: About MiaoMiaoWu X
about.description: MiaoMiaoWu X is an enhanced version of MiaoMiaoWu, focused on remote server management and Xray configuration

whatIs.heading: What is MiaoMiaoWu X
whatIs.text1: MiaoMiaoWu X (MMWX) is an enhanced version based on MiaoMiaoWu. It retains all existing subscription management features while adding advanced capabilities such as remote server management, full Xray service management, certificate management, and package management.
whatIs.text2: It uses a Master-Agent architecture where the master node communicates with Agents on remote servers via WebSocket/HTTP, enabling unified management of multiple servers.

overview.heading: Overview
overview.text: The certificate management module automatically issues and renews TLS certificates via the ACME protocol. It supports multiple DNS providers and automatically syncs certificates to remote servers.

applyCert.heading: Apply for Certificate
applyCert.step3: 3. Select a DNS provider and enter credentials
applyCert.step5: 5. After submission, the system automatically completes DNS verification and certificate issuance

autoRenew.heading: Auto Renewal
autoRenew.text: The system automatically renews certificates before expiration. After successful renewal, certificate files are automatically updated on all remote servers using the certificate.

useCases.heading: Use Cases
useCases.item1: - VLESS/VMess/Trojan + TLS inbounds require a TLS certificate
useCases.item2: - Hysteria2 / AnyTLS inbounds require a TLS certificate
useCases.item3: - REALITY inbounds do not need certificates (uses target site's certificate)

upload.heading: Webhook certificate upload (Certimate integration)
upload.intro: MiaomiaowuX exposes an HTTP endpoint that accepts certificates pushed from external issuance systems (e.g. Certimate) via webhook. Once stored, it auto-deploys to all remote servers tagged with that domain.
upload.endpointAuth: Auth: Authorization: Bearer <token> or MM-Authorization: <token> (Global API Token from System Settings, or a personal admin token issued in the API Token page)
upload.fieldDomain: domain — main domain bound to the certificate (e.g. *.example.com or a.example.com)
upload.fieldCertPem: cert_pem — certificate PEM text; accepts raw PEM (starts with -----BEGIN) or base64 (legacy UI format)
upload.fieldKeyPem: key_pem — private key PEM text; same dual format support
upload.behavior1: Existing same-domain cert → update the record (cert_path / key_path / expiry) and trigger auto-deploy
upload.behavior2: First-time upload → create a manual / no-deploy record; deploy target can be switched in the UI later
upload.behavior3: Success returns {success: true, certificate_id: N}; failure returns {success: false, message: "..."}
upload.certimateText: Certimate's webhook deployer substitutes ${CERTIMATE_DEPLOYER_*} template variables with the actual cert content (raw PEM, no base64). MiaomiaowuX auto-detects the -----BEGIN prefix and accepts both raw PEM and the legacy base64 upload paths.
upload.curlText: POST the file contents directly without base64 transcoding. Replace the paths with your actual cert locations.

embeddedXray.title: Embedded Xray
embeddedXray.description: Run the Xray core directly within the Agent process, unlocking rate limit push and real-time traffic control (PRO)
embeddedXray.overviewText: Embedded Xray is a PRO feature that runs the Xray core as a library directly within the Agent process, replacing the traditional external Xray service. In embedded mode, the Agent has full control over the Xray core, enabling advanced features not possible in external mode, such as real-time rate limit push, device limits, automatic rate limit rules, and precise online user tracking.
embeddedXray.proNote: Embedded Xray is a PRO feature that requires a valid license with the limiter feature.
embeddedXray.cmpRunMethodEmbedded: Embedded as library in Agent process
embeddedXray.realTimeRateLimitText: The master pushes rate limit configurations to the embedded Xray Agent via WebSocket. The Agent updates rate limit rules instantly without restarting. Each user on each inbound has an independent rate bucket.
embeddedXray.visionRateLimitText: The XTLS-RPRX-VISION protocol uses zero-copy (splice) optimization, making normal traffic interception ineffective. Embedded mode registers a Vision Limiter Hook to insert a rate limiting layer before splice, enabling precise rate control on Vision connections. When no rate limit is set (no rate bucket), the Hook returns null and the connection uses the original splice path with zero overhead.
embeddedXray.autoRateLimitText: Embedded mode includes a built-in SpeedMonitor that can be configured with automatic rate limit rules. When user traffic behavior triggers thresholds, temporary rate limits are automatically applied.
embeddedXray.sustainedDesc: Triggered when user speed continuously exceeds threshold for a specified duration. Example: 5 seconds above 100 Mbps -> limit to 50 Mbps for 30 seconds
embeddedXray.burstDesc: Triggered when overspeed count reaches threshold within a time window. Example: exceeding 80 Mbps 3 times within 10 seconds -> limit to 40 Mbps for 60 seconds
embeddedXray.onlineTrackingText: Embedded mode precisely tracks each user's online IP addresses and connection count. When a user's connections reach the device limit, new connections are rejected. Existing connections are not affected. Online information is periodically reported to the master via WebSocket.
embeddedXray.customDispatcherText: Embedded mode replaces the default Xray dispatcher with a custom dispatcher that intercepts all outbound traffic. The custom dispatcher integrates the rate limiting module, applying rate limits to each connection's write operations via RateWriter while maintaining full traffic statistics capability.
embeddedXray.enableMethod1Text: On the master's server management page, you can directly switch the server's Xray mode. After switching, the Agent will automatically restart and run in the new mode.
embeddedXray.enableMethod1Step2: 2. Click settings on the target server
embeddedXray.enableMethod2Text: Edit the Agent's configuration file config.yaml directly:
embeddedXray.enableMethod2Restart: Restart the Agent service after modification:
embeddedXray.startupText: When the Agent starts in embedded mode, it performs the following steps:
embeddedXray.startupStep1: 1. Stop external Xray - automatically runs systemctl stop xray to avoid port conflicts
embeddedXray.startupStep2: 2. Config migration - if external Xray confdir config exists, auto-merge to standard path
embeddedXray.startupStep3: 3. Ensure geodata - check if geoip.dat / geosite.dat exist
embeddedXray.startupStep4: 4. Inject runtime components - inject custom dispatcher, stats module, and policy config into Xray config
embeddedXray.startupStep5: 5. Register Vision Hook - register XTLS Vision zero-copy connection rate limit interceptor
embeddedXray.startupStep6: 6. Start core - create and start embedded Xray instance
embeddedXray.startupStep7: 7. Connect to master - establish WebSocket connection, start receiving rate limit config push
embeddedXray.trafficStatsText: Embedded mode uses Xray's built-in statistics counters to collect traffic data, supporting per-inbound and per-user statistics:
embeddedXray.counterNote: Counters use cumulative mode (non-destructive reads). The Agent periodically reports incremental data to the master.
embeddedXray.noteAutoStop: When switching to embedded mode, the Agent automatically stops the external Xray service. After switching back to external mode, ensure the Xray service starts normally.
embeddedXray.noteFixedPath: The embedded mode config path is fixed at /usr/local/etc/xray/config.json and does not auto-detect multiple paths like external mode. Ensure this path is accessible.
embeddedXray.noteLimiterLicense: Rate limit push requires a PRO license with the limiter feature. Without authorization, the Agent ignores rate limit configs pushed by the master.
embeddedXray.noteNoInstall: Embedded mode does not require separate Xray installation - the Agent includes the complete Xray core. Switching modes does not affect existing inbound configurations.
embeddedXray.noteBadge: In the master UI, servers in embedded mode display a blue "Embedded" badge, while external mode shows a gray "External" badge for easy identification.

demo.heading: Try it: external vs embedded Xray

demo.embeddedDesc: xray-core baked into Agent binary, single process. Smaller footprint, easier upgrades.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.description: Frequently asked questions grouped by topic.

carpool.heading: Carpool guide

metering.heading: Traffic metering
metering.text: MiaomiaowuX does NOT use a port-based metering scheme (the way TrafficDog does). Instead, it uses Xray's native inbound/outbound + user accounting. The same port on the same server can be shared by many users; each user's traffic is tracked by their client.email identity. No need to allocate a separate port per user.

step1.heading: 1. Server Management — add a server
step1.text: Add a server in Server Management with name, IP, domain. The master generates a pairing token; deploy the Agent on the target machine. See the Install Agent doc for full flow.
step1.caption: Server Management — cards show status / live speed / traffic / Xray actions

step2.heading: 2. Node Management — add nodes
step2.text: Nodes can be auto-synced from server inbounds (recommended) or added manually. A single server can host multiple protocols (VLESS / Trojan / Shadowsocks / Hysteria2 / AnyTLS, etc.); all of them can carpool the same set of users.
step2.caption: Node Management — inbound nodes grouped by server, batch edit / toggle / sync

step3.heading: 3. Package Management — create package, pick nodes
step3.text: In Package Management, create a package with quota / cycle / metering mode (one-way / two-way), and tick the nodes this package can access on the right (unticked = all). A package is a permission group — carpool members share the same package.
step3.caption: Create package dialog — params on the left, associated nodes on the right

step4.heading: 4. User Management — add users, bind package
step4.text: Add users in User Management, then click Manage Package on a user's row to bind the package from step 3 with an expiration date. Repeat for each carpool member.
step4.caption: User Management — list of all users and their package status
step4.bindCaption: Manage package dialog — pick package + expiration + reset cycle

carpool.tips: Tip: each user's credentials (uuid, password, etc.) are unique and are injected into the node config by MiaomiaowuX when the user fetches their subscription. So sharing a single node with multiple users requires zero manual work — finish the 4 steps above, and every user's subscription gets their own credential automatically; traffic is metered per username.

tgbot.heading: TG Bot & Mini App
tgbot.text: With mmwX-tgbot connected to Telegram, carpool members can check account / traffic / subscription right inside Telegram, and admins can issue redeem codes and bind users from there. The Mini App provides a login-free mobile dashboard, lighter than the web console.
tgbot.adminCaption: Admin view: redeem codes / user binding / traffic summary (mobile)
tgbot.userCaption: Regular user view: account / traffic / subscription (mobile)

mcp.heading: OpenClaw & Hermes (MCP)
mcp.text: MiaomiaowuX has a built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. Any MCP-compatible AI Agent — OpenClaw, Hermes Agent, Claude Code, Cursor, etc. — can connect and let you operate nodes / subscriptions / traffic / servers / users / packages in natural language. For example, tell the agent "create a 100GB package for alice and bind it" and steps 3 & 4 finish automatically.
mcp.caption: System Settings → API Token: paste this token into the mcp_servers config of OpenClaw / Hermes

carpool.description: Carpool from zero in 4 steps, plus extra tools (TG Bot / Mini App / MCP).

installDeploy.heading: Installation & Deployment
installDeploy.q1: What's the difference between MiaoMiaoWu X and MiaoMiaoWu?
installDeploy.a1: MiaoMiaoWu X is an extended version of MiaoMiaoWu with added remote server management, Xray inbound/outbound management, and protocol connectivity testing. MiaoMiaoWu focuses on subscription management and traffic monitoring, while MiaoMiaoWu X provides complete server management capabilities.
installDeploy.a2: Master: Linux x86_64, 1 CPU core and 512MB RAM minimum. Agent: Linux x86_64, requires Xray-core. Supports both Docker and binary deployment.
installDeploy.q3: Can the master and Agent run on the same machine?
installDeploy.a3: Yes, but they need different ports. The master uses port 12889 by default, and the Agent needs a different port.
installDeploy.description: Common questions about installing, deploying the Agent, and upgrading.

commonOps.heading: Common Operations
commonOps.q1: How to add an inbound / node?
commonOps.a1: Go to "Node Management", click the "Add Node" button in the top right corner. In the wizard popup, select server, protocol, transport/security, then save. The system will create the corresponding Xray inbound and automatically sync it as a node in the list.
commonOps.q2: How to specify a node's outbound (set up landing)?
commonOps.a2: In "Node Management", click the bidirectional arrow button on a node row. In the popup dialog, you can either select an existing node as the outbound, or create a new landing inbound on a server (the system auto-configures inbound, outbound, and routing rules). This is the entry point for chain proxy / landing relay.
commonOps.q3: How to share accounts with others?
commonOps.a3p2: 1) Create a package: In "Package Management", create a new package - set traffic quota, reset cycle (monthly/custom), speed limit (optional), device limit (optional), and select which nodes the package includes.
commonOps.a3p3: 2) Create a user and bind: In "User Management", create a new user and bind the package. The system automatically generates a subscription link for the user - they can use any client to pull this link and see the nodes in the package.
commonOps.a3p4: When quota is exceeded, nodes are automatically removed from subscription or speed is limited based on your package configuration.
commonOps.q4: I migrated from MiaoMiaoWu (mmw), why can't I see the original features?
commonOps.a4: You need to enable the "MiaoMiaoWu Features Toggle" in "System Settings". Once enabled, MiaoMiaoWu-related menus (subscription probe, subscription generation, etc.) will appear in the top bar, coexisting with MiaoMiaoWu X features like server management, packages, and templates.
commonOps.q5: I forgot the admin password — how do I reset it?
commonOps.a5p1: MiaoMiaoWuX provides no remote channel to reset the admin password (no API, no license service, no remote command). This is an intentional safety constraint — any centralized remote channel, once compromised, would let an attacker take over every user's admin account.
commonOps.a5p2: The correct approach is to SSH into your server and run the script reset-admin-password.sh shipped with the project. It auto-detects your deployment (systemd one-click install / Docker), locates the database, lists admin accounts for you to pick, asks for a new password, bcrypt-hashes it into the DB, and restarts mmwx.
commonOps.a5p5: If GitHub is unreachable, find the script in your local mmwx source tree at scripts/reset-admin-password.sh and run it directly with bash.
commonOps.a5p6: The script will: 1) auto-detect mmwx.db (default /etc/mmwx/data, walks Docker volumes, falls back to /proc cwd of the mmwx process); 2) install missing sqlite3 / htpasswd via apt/yum/apk; 3) list all active admins (prompts to pick when there are multiple); 4) ask for the new password twice (hidden input); 5) auto-backup the DB (.bak-timestamp); 6) write the bcrypt hash, and also disable 2FA in case the TOTP device was lost along with the password; 7) auto-restart mmwx (systemctl / docker restart).
commonOps.a5p7: Once it finishes, log in with the new password. Then go to "System Settings → Change Password" and set a fresh password yourself, and create a second admin under "User Management" as a backup.
commonOps.description: Everyday operations — subscriptions, nodes, quotas, etc.

serverManagement.heading: Server Management
serverManagement.q1: What to do if the remote server can't connect?
serverManagement.a1: Check the following: 1) Is the Agent running? 2) Is the firewall allowing the Agent port? 3) Is the Token correct? 4) Is the network reachable? Check connection status and error messages on the server management page.
serverManagement.q2: How to choose between WebSocket, HTTP, and Pull connection modes?
serverManagement.a2: WebSocket suits most scenarios with good real-time performance. HTTP suits unstable network environments. Pull mode has the Agent actively pull instructions, suitable for Agents behind NAT. Auto mode automatically selects the best method.
serverManagement.description: Remote server: adding, tokens, status, connectivity.

protocolInbound.heading: Protocols & Inbounds
protocolInbound.a1: Recommended: VLESS + TCP + REALITY + XTLS-Vision. No domain or certificate needed, best performance. For CDN relay, use VLESS/VMess + WebSocket + TLS.
protocolInbound.q2: What's the difference between REALITY and TLS?
protocolInbound.a2: TLS requires a domain and certificate, REALITY does not. REALITY disguises itself as the target website's TLS handshake to avoid detection, offering higher security. However, REALITY does not support CDN relay.
protocolInbound.a3: Xray-core has removed flow (XTLS-Vision) support for Trojan. For Vision flow control, please use the VLESS protocol.

subClient.heading: Subscriptions & Clients
subClient.q1: Subscription link won't open or nodes are empty?
subClient.a1: Check: 1) Is the Token correct? 2) Is the user bound to a package? 3) Are there available nodes? 4) Are nodes disabled?
subClient.q2: Trojan nodes won't connect in mihomo?
subClient.a2: In mihomo, Trojan uses the sni field instead of servername. The system handles this automatically. If issues persist, check if the certificate domain matches.
subClient.description: Subscription URL, client compatibility, format conversion.

installAgent.title: Agent Deployment
installAgent.description: Deploy MMWX Agent on remote servers with a one-click command

overview.heading: Overview
overview.text: Agent deployment on remote servers requires no manual download or configuration. After adding a server on the master, the system automatically generates a one-click install command that can be run on the remote server to complete the entire deployment.

tokenWarning.title: Important: each server must use a unique agent_token
tokenWarning.body1: Do NOT copy one agent config (same token) to multiple servers and run them in parallel. Each token maps to a single server record on the master; reusing it across machines triggers a reconnect storm — the two agents keep evicting each other and the master is forced to handle several database writes per second.
tokenWarning.body2: The damage is not contained to the conflicting server: the write storm saturates the master's SQLite write queue, causing heartbeat updates from unrelated servers to time out. Those servers get incorrectly marked offline and degraded to HTTP/Pull mode. The master now auto-locks the token to the first-connecting IP for 60s and rejects same-token auth from other IPs — see "agent_token reuse detected" warnings in the logs. Fix: add and use a unique token per server.

xrayMode.heading: Xray mode
xrayMode.text: MiaomiaowuX Agent now supports two Xray runtime modes; pick one via the xray_mode parameter of install.sh. Try not to switch back and forth — stop all nodes before switching.
xrayMode.embeddedDesc: Xray-core is embedded inside the Agent — they share the same process; no standalone /usr/local/bin/xray needed; upgrading the Agent upgrades Xray. Lower footprint, fits stable setups that do not need manual xray surgery.
xrayMode.externalDesc: Installs standalone xray via the official XTLS/Xray-install script; Agent controls it over gRPC. Xray can be upgraded independently, follows the official file layout. The one-click installer defaults to this mode.
xrayMode.note: Note: Hysteria2 / AnyTLS and other newer protocols are supported in both modes. In embedded mode, startup errors are logged with the [EmbeddedXray] prefix in the Agent log.

step1.heading: Step 1: Add Server on Master
step1.item1: 1. Log in to the master management panel
step1.item3: 3. Click "Add Server" and enter server name and IP
step1.item4: 4. After saving, the system automatically generates the install command for that server

step2.heading: Step 2: Copy the One-Click Install Command
step2.text: Find the "One-Click Install Command" in the server details and click the copy button. The command format is as follows:
step2.note: Each server has a unique Token. The command already includes the server's authentication info and master address.
step2.paramToken: Server pairing token from the master (required)
step2.paramStealSelf: Set to 1 to auto-install nginx and enable steal-self mode
step2.paramListenPort: Starting listen port; auto-increments on conflict up to 20 retries

step3.heading: Step 3: Run on the Remote Server
step3.text: SSH into the remote server and run the copied command with root privileges. The script automatically completes the following 6 steps:
step3.desc1: Stop existing mmwx service if running
step3.desc3: Register as system service with auto-start on boot
step3.desc5: Enable and start the mmwx service
step3.desc6: Check service status and output result

docker.heading: Docker Deployment
docker.intro: Besides the one-click bare-metal + systemd script, you can also pull the Docker image directly. The image ships with embedded xray-core + nginx + all dependencies — pull and run, no in-container installation needed.
docker.hostAlertTitle: Must run with host network mode
docker.hostAlertText1: Xray inbound ports are added dynamically by the master UI — bridge mode would force you to update -p mappings every time a new port appears; the agent's own listening port for the master's reverse connection also needs the host network stack.
docker.hostAlertText2: The container entrypoint checks the default gateway IP on startup — if it matches the Docker bridge default range (172.17.x.x / 172.18.x.x) the container exits with an error. Always use --network host or network_mode: host.
docker.runText: Good for manual / one-off testing. The token comes from the master "Add Server" form — same token as the one-click script generates.
docker.runNote: All 4 volumes (config / xray-config / nginx-cert / nginx-servers) are optional (the container has its own dirs). Mapping them out mainly helps with debugging or preserving state across image upgrades.
docker.composeText: Recommended for production — docker compose pull / up -d makes future upgrades smoother.
docker.constraint1Text: Required. Bridge mode is rejected by the entrypoint — bypassing is not recommended.
docker.constraint2Text: The image has no external xray binary, only embedded mode is supported. Select "embedded" when adding the server in the master UI (requires PRO license; the agent itself does not verify signatures).
docker.constraint3Text: Pre-installed via apt + symlinks compatible with hardcoded paths. The master's "install nginx" request returns "already installed" immediately, no install action triggered.
docker.constraint4Text: Not recommended. May hit port conflicts. Debug only:

autoXray.heading: Automatic Xray Installation
autoXray.text: The installer decides whether to install Xray based on the xray_mode flag. External mode (xray_mode=external, default) installs standalone Xray via XTLS/Xray-install (skipped if already present); the Agent controls it over gRPC. Embedded mode (xray_mode=embedded) does NOT install standalone Xray — xray-core is baked into the Agent binary and started by the Agent process.

configFile.heading: Generated Configuration File
configFile.note: All fields are generated by install.sh based on URL parameters; after manual edits run systemctl restart mmw-agent.

systemdService.heading: Generated systemd Service
systemdService.note: The installer picks the right init system automatically: systemd / OpenRC (Alpine) / nohup+rc.local (LXC fallback).

connectionMode.heading: Connection Mode
connectionMode.text: The one-click installation uses WebSocket connection mode by default. To change it, edit the connection_mode field in /etc/mmw-agent/config.yaml.
connectionMode.wsDesc: Recommended. Agent actively connects to master, maintaining a persistent connection
connectionMode.httpDesc: Master directly calls Agent API, requires Agent port to be reachable
connectionMode.pullDesc: Agent periodically pulls instructions, suitable for NAT environments
connectionMode.autoDesc: Automatically tries WebSocket -> HTTP -> Pull fallback chain

reinstall.heading: Reinstallation
reinstall.text: If the Agent has issues, find the "Reinstall Command" in the server details and run it again. The script automatically stops the old service, reinstalls, and restarts.

builtinNginx.heading: Built-in nginx (read this before enabling HTTPS)
builtinNginx.alertTitle: nginx is pre-installed in the image — no extra setup
builtinNginx.alertText: The MiaoMiaoWu X image already ships nginx, fully managed by the application. Clicking "Deploy HTTPS" in the web UI brings nginx up inside the container — you don't install, configure, or start nginx yourself.
builtinNginx.text1: Because nginx runs INSIDE the container, you must map the container's 443 port to the host for external HTTPS access. The basic example only maps 12889 — without 443, https://your-domain will not reach the container.
builtinNginx.port80: ACME HTTP-01 challenge. Required for issuing / renewing certificates. Can be omitted if you don't enable HTTPS.
builtinNginx.port443: Built-in nginx HTTPS entry. After "Deploy HTTPS" in the UI, this is where external TLS traffic terminates.
builtinNginx.port12889: Master HTTP panel port. Once HTTPS is on, nginx reverse-proxies to 12889, but exposing it externally still helps for bridge-mode debugging.
builtinNginx.text3: If something else on your host already holds 80 / 443, this container will conflict — switch to host networking (--network=host) or move the other apps off those ports.

installDocker.composeNote: By default all ports are mapped. In production without HTTPS, you may drop the 80 / 443 lines and keep only 12889.

mcp.title: Connect AI Agent (MCP)
mcp.description: MiaoMiaoWu X has a built-in MCP service that allows AI Agents like OpenClaw / Hermes to manage your master with natural language
mcp.overviewText1a: MiaoMiaoWu X master has a built-in MCP (Model Context Protocol) service mounted on the
mcp.overviewText1b: route (streamable-HTTP). Any MCP-compatible AI Agent (OpenClaw, Hermes Agent, Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) can connect directly to manage nodes, subscriptions, traffic, servers, users, packages, and more with natural language.
mcp.overviewText2: Tool calls reuse the master's existing API and authentication. Permissions are identical to your login account: admin tokens can perform admin operations, while regular user tokens calling admin tools will be rejected.
mcp.step1Item1: 1. Log in to the master, go to "Settings -> API Tokens".
mcp.step1Item3Bold: The plaintext is shown only once
mcp.step1Item3Rest: - copy and save it immediately. The page also provides ready-to-copy client configuration snippets.
mcp.step1Item4: Token permissions match the current account; you can delete (revoke) it at any time from the same page.
mcp.hermesHeading: Hermes Agent (~/.hermes/config.yaml, add mcp_servers at top level)
mcp.hermesNote: Restart the Agent after adding (MCP connects at startup). On success, the log will show
mcp.otherClients: Other clients (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) work the same way: enter the
mcp.toolsDesc: 26 tools in total, grouped by capability domain. Write operations are marked, and high-risk operations require explicit confirmation.
mcp.toolsNote: * = High-risk write operation. Agent must include
mcp.toolsNoteSuffix: in parameters to execute, otherwise only returns a confirmation prompt. Extremely dangerous operations like token reset, clearing all nodes, uninstalling xray/nginx, and modifying admin credentials are not exposed.
mcp.skillsDesc: We provide a set of Claude Agent Skills
mcp.skillsDescSuffix: that teach the Agent how to combine the above tools for common operations. Place them in the Agent's skills directory.
mcp.skillOnboard: Full new user onboarding (create user -> package -> bind -> subscription)
mcp.skillAddServer: Onboard new server (register -> install xray/nginx -> sync nodes)
mcp.securityItem1: Token permissions follow the account: before giving full access to an Agent, confirm that the Agent and its entry point (e.g. Telegram bot) are trusted.
mcp.securityItem2a: Minimize blast radius: use client tool filtering (e.g. Hermes
mcp.securityItem2b: ) to only expose read-only tools, or use a regular user token.
mcp.securityItem3: Token plaintext is shown only once. If leaked, delete it at "Settings -> API Tokens" to revoke.
mcp.securityItem4b: double confirmation is a safety net, not a substitute for access control.

nodeRatelimit.title: Node Rate Limiting
nodeRatelimit.description: Set download speed and device limits for users through packages (PRO)
nodeRatelimit.overviewText: Node rate limiting allows administrators to configure download speed limits and maximum concurrent devices for each package. When a user is bound to a package, rate limit rules are automatically pushed to all inbounds associated with that user, enforced at the traffic level by the embedded Xray core.
nodeRatelimit.proNote: Node rate limiting is a PRO feature that requires a valid license. Rate limiting only takes effect on servers using embedded Xray mode; external Xray mode does not support rate limit push.
nodeRatelimit.paramsText: Rate limits are configured through the package management page. Each package can set the following rate limit parameters:
nodeRatelimit.speedLimitDesc: Limits the per-user download speed for this package. Set to 0 for no limit. Internally converted to bytes/s: Mbps x 1000000 / 8
nodeRatelimit.deviceLimitDesc: Limits the maximum number of concurrent online devices per user. Set to 0 for no limit. New connections exceeding the limit are rejected.
nodeRatelimit.userOverrideText: In addition to package-level rate limit configuration, individual user-level rate limit overrides are supported, with higher priority than package settings.
nodeRatelimit.userOverrideDesc2: speed_limit_override and device_limit_override set for individual users in user management
nodeRatelimit.packageDesc: speed_limit_mbps and device_limit from the user's package
nodeRatelimit.autoPushText: Rate limit configurations are automatically pushed to the Agent in the following scenarios, no manual action needed:
nodeRatelimit.triggerConnectDesc: After an embedded Xray mode Agent connects to master, automatically pushes rate limit config for all users on that server
nodeRatelimit.triggerPackageChangeDesc: After user binds or switches package, re-push that user's rate limit config
nodeRatelimit.triggerUserToggleDesc: Removes rate limit config when user is disabled (inbounds also disabled), re-pushes when enabled
nodeRatelimit.triggerUserDeleteDesc: Pushes removal of that user's rate limit config
nodeRatelimit.triggerExpireDesc: Automatically disables all inbounds for that user and removes rate limit config
nodeRatelimit.configStep1: 1. Ensure the target server uses embedded Xray mode (external mode does not support rate limiting)
nodeRatelimit.configStep2: 2. Go to the "Package Management" page, create or edit a package
nodeRatelimit.configStep3: 3. Set "Download Speed Limit (Mbps)" and "Max Devices" in the package form
nodeRatelimit.configStep4: 4. After saving the package, bind users to it
nodeRatelimit.configStep5: 5. Rate limit config will be automatically pushed to all embedded Xray servers associated with the user
nodeRatelimit.packageParamsText: Packages include the following configurations in addition to rate limits:
nodeRatelimit.pkgTrafficDesc: Total traffic quota for the package, disabled when exceeded
nodeRatelimit.pkgNodesDesc: Restrict which nodes this package can access
nodeRatelimit.pkgTrafficModeDesc: Unidirectional (max of upload or download) or bidirectional (upload + download)
nodeRatelimit.noteEmbeddedOnly: Rate limiting only works on servers in embedded Xray mode. Servers in external Xray mode will not receive rate limit push, and users on those servers are not speed-limited.
nodeRatelimit.noteReconnect: After modifying package rate limit parameters, you need to wait for the next push opportunity (e.g. server reconnect) for changes to take effect on already-connected Agents. It's recommended to manually restart the Agent or wait for heartbeat auto-push.
nodeRatelimit.noteDeviceTracking: Device limiting works by tracking each user's active connection count. When the connection count reaches the limit, new connections are rejected while existing connections are not affected.

demo.description: Click either button below to open the «package limits» or «user limits» dialog. All changes are saved locally in the demo and reflected in the «effective view» table below.

concept.heading: Two-layer limits + per-node fine-grained control
concept.text: Package general applies to «every node × every user bound to it»; package per-node / user global / user per-node override it in layers. Each layer is detected by «is the input empty»; non-empty (including 0) counts as explicitly set.
concept.formula: effective = userNode ?? userGlobal ?? packageNode ?? packageGlobal ?? unlimited

nodeSpeedtest.title: Node Speed Test
nodeSpeedtest.description: Test node download speed and real connection latency using mihomo core, supporting master local and home speed test endpoints with single/multi-thread switching
nodeSpeedtest.overviewText: Node speed testing uses the mihomo core to measure actual download throughput and latency through single-node proxies. Test source, thread count, and test type can be freely combined, with results saved on the server for historical review.
nodeSpeedtest.localText: The master automatically downloads the mihomo core and tests speed from the master's network environment. Works out of the box, suitable when the master is deployed at home or on the target network.
nodeSpeedtest.homeText: Run a speed test endpoint on your home computer/server that connects to the master via reverse WebSocket, testing speed from a real home network perspective. Suitable when the master is in a data center but you want to know actual home exit speeds.
nodeSpeedtest.speedTestText: Downloads a large Google file through the proxy for about 8 seconds, calculating Mbps from actual bytes/time; also runs a single www.gstatic.com/generate_204 latency test.
nodeSpeedtest.speedTestTrigger: Trigger: Click the inline Gauge icon (orange) or the top "Batch Speed Test" button.
nodeSpeedtest.latencyTestText: Only runs Cloudflare 204 multi-sampling (cp.cloudflare.com/generate_204), takes the average of the fastest 2 out of 3 samples, removing cold-start first packet. No file download, results in seconds.
nodeSpeedtest.latencyTestTrigger: Trigger: Click the Zap icon button in the latency column. Click again to re-test.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep1: Go to "Node Management", click the "Node Speed Test" button at the top to open the speed test workbench.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep2: Select the test source at the top (default "Master Local"; if you've configured a home test endpoint, you can select it). The selection is saved in browser local storage and auto-restored next time.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep3: Select thread mode (single/multi-thread, 8 concurrent): single thread shows worst-path performance; multi-thread aggregates bandwidth, closer to actual available bandwidth. Thread selection is also cached locally.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep4: Each table row shows protocol / node name / server / speed / latency / exit IP.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep4a: Click the inline Gauge icon for speed test (includes latency).
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep4b: Click the Zap button in the latency column for real connection latency (Cloudflare 204); tested values show ms, click again to re-test.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep4c: Check multiple + click top "Batch Speed Test" to run multiple speed tests at once.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep5: Speed tests are asynchronous: clicking returns immediately, shows spinner while running, auto-refreshes results on completion (1.5s polling, near-instant response). Results persist even if you leave and return.
nodeSpeedtest.usageStep6: Click inline "History" to view the node's historical speed test records.
nodeSpeedtest.collapseNote: The speed test workbench collapses to a floating button on the right side when clicking outside or pressing Esc (preventing accidental closure and lost progress). Click to re-expand; click the X in the top right to actually close. Re-opening returns to the main view (not stuck on the "manage test endpoints" subpage).
nodeSpeedtest.configStep1: In the speed test workbench, click "Test Endpoint Management" -> enter a name -> click "Create" to get a one-time pairing token and one-click install commands (Linux/macOS + Windows). The token is shown only once - be sure to copy and save it first.
nodeSpeedtest.configStep2: Run the command on your home machine:
nodeSpeedtest.configStep3: The test endpoint automatically connects to the master via reverse WebSocket (WSS) after startup - no public IP needed at home. Once connected, it appears as "Online" in the test source list, select it to use.
nodeSpeedtest.configStep4: The test endpoint auto-reconnects after disconnection. When marked "Offline", it cannot be selected (already-selected will auto-fallback to master local).
nodeSpeedtest.capabilitiesText: The home speed test endpoint has the same capabilities as master local: download speed test + real connection latency test + single/multi-thread switching + exit IP display. All capabilities are dispatched through the same WebSocket reverse connection, with results written to the master's history table.
nodeSpeedtest.binaryNote: The speed test endpoint binary is released at
nodeSpeedtest.binaryNoteSuffix: , supporting Linux / Windows / macOS. The one-click install script automatically downloads the platform-specific binary and configures systemd (Linux) or auto-start (Windows).
nodeSpeedtest.scenario1What: Max bandwidth of node at master's data center
nodeSpeedtest.scenario1Explain: Aggregates 8 download streams, approaches physical bandwidth limit
nodeSpeedtest.scenario2What: How fast single-stream apps (video/file copy) can run
nodeSpeedtest.scenario2Explain: Reflects real speed of a single TCP connection under congestion control
nodeSpeedtest.scenario3What: How stuttery YouTube is at home
nodeSpeedtest.scenario3Choice: Home Test Endpoint + Multi-thread + Speed Test
nodeSpeedtest.scenario3Explain: Tests from home exit through proxy, closest to actual user experience
nodeSpeedtest.scenario4What: Just want to quickly check latency without waiting 8s for download
nodeSpeedtest.scenario4Choice: Click Zap button in latency column (Real Connection Latency)
nodeSpeedtest.scenario4Explain: 3 Cloudflare 204 samples, average of fastest 2, results in 2-4 seconds
nodeSpeedtest.scenario5What: Verify exit IP is as expected
nodeSpeedtest.scenario5Choice: Run any speed test, check the "Exit IP" column
nodeSpeedtest.scenario5Explain: Real outbound IP echoed through the proxy
nodeSpeedtest.note1: All tasks within the same test source (master or a specific test endpoint) run serially to avoid bandwidth contention causing inaccurate results; batch testing many nodes takes longer.
nodeSpeedtest.note2: Speed tests default to time-based (about 8 seconds) rather than fixed size, so slow nodes won't timeout because bytes weren't fully downloaded.
nodeSpeedtest.note3: Real connection latency uses Cloudflare 204 (global edge nodes) with broader coverage than gstatic; the first test requires mihomo cold start, so sampling removes the slowest one for more stable results.
nodeSpeedtest.note4: Node speed testing is a PRO feature that requires a valid
nodeSpeedtest.note5: History records accumulate per node; click History in the node row to view; supports sorting by speed/latency/time.

nodes.title: Node Management
nodes.description: Manage all proxy nodes — three sources (Xray inbounds auto-sync / remote servers / external subscriptions), enable/disable, rename, sort, group, plus tunnel forwarding and visual online-status indicators.
nodes.overviewText: Nodes are the subscription system's representation of inbound configurations. After each inbound is created, the system automatically generates corresponding nodes for subscription distribution.
nodes.sourceXrayDesc: Protocol inbounds created via the inbound wizard
nodes.sourceExternalDesc: Nodes imported from external subscription links
nodes.autoSyncText: When inbound configurations change, the system automatically triggers node sync via the event bus. The sync process converts inbound configurations to mihomo/Clash compatible proxy node format.
nodes.opRenameDesc: Customize the node's display name in subscriptions
nodes.opSortDesc: Adjust the node's order in subscriptions
nodes.opGroupDesc: Assign nodes to different proxy groups
nodes.opTagsDesc: Since v0.2.3 nodes support multiple tags: the edit dialog lets you add / remove tags freely; frontend filters and package selected_tags match if any tag hits. Existing single-tag nodes are auto-backfilled to a single-element array.
nodes.tunnelText: Tunnel (dokodemo-door) inbounds forward traffic from one server's port to another node. Tunnels don't appear in the node list themselves - they're managed from the "Tunnel Management" entry at the top of node management (aggregated across all remote/shared servers).
nodes.tunnelForwardText: When adding an inbound, select Tunnel protocol, then choose "Forward Existing Node" - it automatically fills in the forwarding address/port/network type based on the selected node. After creation, a companion node is auto-generated: named "Original Node Name | Tunnel", with the inbound tag of the tunnel. The Clash config clones the original node but changes the server address and port to the tunnel server's IP and listen port - so clients connecting to the tunnel server use the forwarding chain.
nodes.tunnelIndicatorText: If a node is being forwarded by a tunnel, it shows a "Forwarded by Tunnel" tag in the node list. Hovering shows which server's tunnel is forwarding it.
nodes.tunnelAdminNote: Tunnel management is an admin feature; also works with received shared servers. Deleting a tunnel also cleans up its companion node.
nodes.note1: Nodes auto-generated from inbounds are automatically updated when the inbound is modified
nodes.note2: Manually renamed nodes will not be overwritten by auto sync
nodes.note3: Disabled nodes will not appear in any subscription output
nodes.note4: After a node resolves to IP, you can one-click "Restore Domain" to restore the original domain before resolution (does not affect the node's server)
nodes.note5: New nodes default to their owning server's name as the tag, making it easy to filter by server. Legacy tags like "manual entry" are left untouched.

screenshot.caption: Node Management — inbound nodes grouped by server, supports batch edit / toggle / sync

overview.heading: Overview
overview.text: Packages define the traffic quota, validity period, and accessible node scope for users. Each user can be bound to one package.

rateLimitDevices.heading: Rate Limit & Devices
rateLimitDevices.text: Packages can set speed limits (Mbps) and device limits; individual user overrides can also be set in user management (user-level overrides package-level).
rateLimitDevices.rateLimitText: Set the limit in Mbps, 0 means unlimited. Once set, the Agent-side rate limiter enforces it on user connections; the UI provides unit conversion hints.
rateLimitDevices.autoRateLimitText: You can enable auto rate limiting on quota exceed: when user traffic exceeds quota, speed is automatically reduced (instead of cutting subscription), and automatically released when traffic resets next month or quota is restored.

trafficBilling.heading: Traffic Statistics & Billing Multiplier
trafficBilling.text: The system uses the Xray traffic collector to track each user's traffic in real-time. When a user's billed traffic exceeds the package quota, the subscription automatically stops returning nodes (or reduces speed via auto rate limiting).
trafficBilling.item1: - The underlying user_traffic table stores raw traffic (upload + download), tracked by node and user separately
trafficBilling.item2: - Packages can select billing direction: unidirectional (oneway, x1) or bidirectional (twoway, x2)
trafficBilling.item3: - Billed traffic = raw traffic (upload + download) x multiplier; quota exceedance is judged by billed traffic
trafficBilling.item4: - Traffic resets monthly; administrators can manually reset user traffic
trafficBilling.displayNote: The "Used Traffic" shown on the homepage and user list shows per-server billing figures for admins (unlimited servers are marked separately), and for users shows billed traffic converted by their package multiplier.

demo.description: 4 mock packages — create, edit, delete; each picks bound nodes / quota / cycle / direction-based metering.

dialog.desc: Basic params on the left, node multi-select on the right (none ticked = all visible).

protocolHysteria2.overviewText: Hysteria2 is based on QUIC (UDP), optimized for high-latency, high-packet-loss networks. Requires a TLS certificate.
protocolHysteria2.prereq1: TLS certificate required (can be auto-issued via certificate management)
protocolHysteria2.xrayConfigText: In Xray-core, Hysteria2 uses protocol: "hysteria" with version: 2. Authentication uses the auth field (not password).

protocolMatrix.title: Protocol Matrix
protocolMatrix.description: All protocol x transport x security combinations supported by MiaoMiaoWu X
protocolMatrix.fullMatrixText: Below are all available combinations (17 total) that have been tested and verified with mihomo connectivity tests.
protocolMatrix.noteAnytlsReality: Backend supported, but no Clash/Mihomo/sing-box client supports it
protocolMatrix.deprecatedText: The following combinations have been deprecated or removed in Xray-core:
protocolMatrix.deprecated1: HTTP/H2 transport: migrated to XHTTP stream-one (H2 & H3)
protocolMatrix.deprecated2: Trojan + Flow (XTLS-Vision): Xray-core has removed flow support for Trojan
protocolMatrix.compat1: Trojan in mihomo uses the sni field (not servername)
protocolMatrix.compat2: XHTTP requires xhttp-opts to include headers: {}, with mode at the top level
protocolMatrix.compat3: REALITY's short-id and public-key go in reality-opts
protocolMatrix.compat4: Hysteria2 uses the hysteria2 type (not hysteria)

protocolShadowsocks.overviewText: Shadowsocks is a classic proxy protocol supporting both AEAD and SS2022 encryption methods. Simple to configure with good compatibility.
protocolShadowsocks.ss2022PasswordText: SS2022 uses a combined format of server password + client password. In mihomo/Clash, the password format is serverPassword:clientPassword.

protocolAnytls.overviewText: AnyTLS is a TLS-based proxy protocol that layers padding + framing + built-in multiplexing on top of TLS to resist TLS-in-TLS DPI. MiaomiaowuX implements it by cherry-picking xray-core PR #5907 into the embedded xray kernel; per-user traffic stats / rate limit / device count limits all reuse the existing xray dispatcher path, sharing the same multi-user pipeline with VLESS / Trojan.
protocolAnytls.tlsDesc: Recommended; requires a domain + TLS cert; existing wildcard certs can be auto-matched
protocolAnytls.realityDesc: Backend supported but no client support: mihomo / clash / sing-box all do NOT support AnyTLS-REALITY; expert mode only
protocolAnytls.client2: mihomo / Clash.Meta (TLS only; REALITY explicitly NOT planned per upstream docs)
protocolAnytls.paddingSchemeText: AnyTLS uses padding rules to obfuscate packet sizes; configured at the inbound level, one rule per line. MiaomiaowuX pre-fills the default 9-line scheme from PR #5907 when creating an inbound; submit as-is for normal use, or edit in the Protocol Settings section. Leave empty to let the server fall back to its built-in default.
protocolAnytls.note1: Auth field is settings.users[].password (NOT clients[]); both mmw-agent and the master subscription generator handle this distinction
protocolAnytls.note2: AnyTLS-REALITY is not on any major client's roadmap (Clash/Mihomo/sing-box); Simple mode defaults the security layer to TLS
protocolAnytls.note3: Inbound wizard Simple mode + TLS auto-matches the server's domain against wildcard certs in Certificate Management (e.g. a.example.com matches *.example.com)
protocolAnytls.note4: Mihomo node fields use password + sni (same as Trojan), produced automatically by the master's inboundToClashProxy

protocolTrojan.overviewText: The Trojan protocol mimics HTTPS traffic and uses password authentication. In Xray-core, it supports TLS and REALITY security layers.
protocolTrojan.note1: Xray-core has removed flow (XTLS-Vision) support for Trojan
protocolTrojan.note2: In mihomo, Trojan uses the sni field instead of servername

protocolVless.overviewText: VLESS is Xray's primary protocol. It is lightweight with no encryption overhead (relies on transport layer encryption) and supports the most transport and security layer combinations.
protocolVless.realityDesc: Recommended, no domain or certificate required
protocolVless.visionText: XTLS-Vision is a flow control mode exclusive to VLESS, enabled via flow: xtls-rprx-vision. It reduces TLS-in-TLS characteristics, improving detection resistance. Only supports TCP transport.

protocolVmess.overviewText: VMess is the native V2Ray protocol with built-in encryption. Compared to VLESS, it has additional encryption overhead but wider compatibility.

remoteServers.title: Remote Servers
remoteServers.description: Add and manage remote servers, Master-Agent architecture
remoteServers.overviewText: MiaoMiaoWu X uses a Master-Agent architecture. The master communicates with Agents on remote servers over the network to install, configure, and manage Xray/Nginx remotely.
remoteServers.addStep1: 1. Go to Service Management, click Add Server
remoteServers.addStep4: 4. Enter domain (optional, for TLS certificates and Nginx camouflage)
remoteServers.addStep5: 5. Select steal mode (tunnel/steal, affects node port generation)
remoteServers.addStep6: 6. System automatically generates Server Token and Agent Token
remoteServers.addStep7: 7. Use Token to deploy Agent on the remote server
remoteServers.deployMethodsText: One-click script (bare metal + systemd) or Docker image (embedded xray + nginx built-in, host network required). Full commands and constraints in
remoteServers.wsDesc: Agent actively connects to master, maintaining a persistent connection. Real-time bidirectional communication, supports scan result push.
remoteServers.httpDesc: Master directly calls Agent HTTP API. Requires Agent port (default 23889) to be reachable from master.
remoteServers.pullDesc: Agent periodically pulls commands from master. Suitable for NAT or firewall-restricted environments.
remoteServers.autoDesc: Automatically tries WebSocket -> HTTP -> Pull fallback chain, selecting the optimal connection method.
remoteServers.serverToken: Server Token: Credential for Agent to connect to master
remoteServers.agentToken: Agent Token: Credential for master to call Agent API
remoteServers.tokenResetNote: Tokens can be reset on the server details page. After reset, Agent will automatically receive the new Token via WebSocket.
remoteServers.trafficSourceText: The "used traffic" and quota limits in the server management list support two data sources. Switch via the "Server Traffic Source" option in the create/edit server dialog. "System NIC traffic" is the recommended default:
remoteServers.trafficSourceSystemDesc: Agent reads physical NIC RX + TX accumulation from /proc/net/dev — includes ALL traffic on the machine: Xray forwarding + SSH + apt update + monitoring/log uploads + tunnel overhead, etc. Matches the NIC billing meter shown on VPS provider panels. Excludes virtual NICs (WARP / Tailscale / Docker / wireguard).
remoteServers.trafficSourceXrayDesc: Aggregates inbound + outbound across all nodes on this server (SUM(uplink + downlink)). Only counts traffic that goes through Xray. Matches the Node View metric. Suitable for transit-only / pure-forwarding boxes — system traffic is negligible, and the number more closely reflects "actual user consumption".
remoteServers.trafficSourceSwitchLabel: Switching = automatic history migration, continuous display value
remoteServers.trafficSourceSwitchNote: Xray → System: the master automatically copies the Xray cumulative + daily-snapshot history to the system dimension. The displayed "used traffic" stays the same the instant you switch. After that, system mode accumulates from real NIC counters and diverges naturally from Xray. Reverse (System → Xray) needs no migration — Xray daily snapshots are always captured regardless of source.
remoteServers.trafficSourceStatsModeNote: The "Stats Mode" option (Upload / Download / Both) applies to both data sources — each can be filtered to one direction or summed.
remoteServers.trafficSourceUnaffected: Node View, User View, and package traffic limit enforcement always use the Xray dimension (system NIC counters can't be split per tag or per user). This option does not affect them.
remoteServers.batchUpgradeText: After the master releases a new version, you can batch upgrade Agents across multiple servers in remote server management without SSH-ing into each one. The upgrade progress is displayed via streaming logs.
remoteServers.shareHeading: Share Server with Other MiaoMiaoWu X
remoteServers.shareText: You can share one of your servers with another MiaoMiaoWu X master, allowing them to manage the server (create inbounds, nodes, etc.) from their panel without directly sharing the Agent token.
remoteServers.shareOwnerText: Generate a share token for the server in server management (with optional prefix) and give it to the other party. Their operations will be forwarded through your master to the server.
remoteServers.shareConsumerText: In Add Server, select Join Shared Server and enter the share token. After joining, the server appears as a federated server in your list, with status refreshed by the owner; inbound / node / Tunnel management are all available.
remoteServers.shareSecurityNote: Communication between master and Agent, and between masters in federation, uses encrypted channels (key negotiation + session caching). Token rotation does not affect online management.

createDialog.desc: Use «{{source}}» as the inbound entrypoint and have the traffic egress through another node. Clients connect to the entrypoint and traffic exits via the chosen node.
createDialog.scopePublic: Public — master, visible to all users
createDialog.scopePublicDesc: Created by the master admin and pushed in subscriptions to every user that can see this node. For shared-server (carpool) scenarios.
createDialog.scopePrivateDesc: A personal routing rule that only affects you; other users are unaffected.

routedOutbound.twoModesText: On the node management page, the button on the right side of each node row opens the Add Landing Node dialog. The Scope selector at the top provides two distinct capabilities:
routedOutbound.prerequisiteText: : Routed outbound for regular users is a feature that requires admin activation. The admin can find the Allow Users to Create Routed Outbounds toggle in System Settings > MiaoMiaoWu Feature Settings. The toggle's right side allows configuring quantity limit (default 2) and daily operation limit (default 5). When disabled, both input fields are inactive.
routedOutbound.prerequisiteDisabledText: When disabled, regular users won't see the route button on node rows. If the API is called through other means:
routedOutbound.prerequisiteErr1: Feature not enabled -> backend returns 403 'Routed outbound feature not available, contact admin to enable'
routedOutbound.prerequisiteErr2: Quantity limit reached -> 403 'Routed outbound quantity limit reached (N/M)'
routedOutbound.prerequisiteErr3: Daily operation limit reached -> 429 'Daily operation limit reached (N/M), please try again tomorrow'
routedOutbound.dialogModesText: The dialog Scope has two modes:
routedOutbound.scopeWholeNodeImpact: All users on the source inbound share the same outbound
routedOutbound.scopeWholeNodeNewNode: No, only changes source node outbound
routedOutbound.scopePerUserImpact: Only users with this child node in their package use this exit
routedOutbound.scopePerUserNewNode: Yes, generates a routed outbound child node
routedOutbound.focusNote: This document focuses on the Per User mode, i.e., Routed Outbound.
routedOutbound.nodeLevelText: The source node's inbound traffic is routed entirely to one exit. All users under that inbound share the same outbound.
routedOutbound.nodeLevelActions: Underlying actions (written to source server xray config):
routedOutbound.nodeLevelAction1: 1. Add outbound, tag = landing-<source inbound tag>-<timestamp>
routedOutbound.nodeLevelAction2: 2. Add routing rule: {inboundTag: [source inbound], outboundTag: previous step's tag}
routedOutbound.nodeLevelUseCase: Use case: All users should use the same exit, e.g., unified relay from frontend entry to backend landing.
routedOutbound.problemHeading: What Problem Does Routed Outbound Solve?
routedOutbound.problemText: In a routing system without a 'user' dimension, an inboundTag -> outboundTag relationship can only have one version, making it impossible to route different users to different exits.
routedOutbound.exampleIntro: Let's illustrate with a specific example. This document uses the following three nodes:
routedOutbound.problemExample: Suppose the admin wants 'AI service traffic to go through the HINET exit' and writes a routing rule:
routedOutbound.problemConflict: But User A has their own Seednet landing server and wants 'their AI traffic to go through Seednet'. The traditional approach would add another rule with the same inbound but different outbound -- xray routing matches in order, stopping at the first match, so the later rule never takes effect.
routedOutbound.problemRoot: Root problem: There is no way to distinguish which rule should apply to 'whose traffic'.
routedOutbound.solutionText: MiaoMiaoWu X registers a dedicated client (unique email) for each user in the parent inbound, with the corresponding routing rule constrained by a user field. Since different users have different emails, multiple rules with the same inbound but different outbounds can coexist.
routedOutbound.solutionAdminNote: Currently, routed outbounds can only be created by admins (API: POST /api/admin/routed-outbound). 'User A routes to Seednet' means the admin creates a routed outbound child node for User A and assigns A to that node via a package, not that the user self-configures routing.
routedOutbound.step1: On the node management page, find the transit node HK-Direct, click the route button at the end of the row
routedOutbound.step2: Select Per User (Routed Outbound) for Scope
routedOutbound.step3: Enter Label (only [a-zA-Z0-9-] allowed, length 2-32), e.g., rout-hinet
routedOutbound.step4: Select the target landing node (e.g., HINET), click Create Routed Outbound
routedOutbound.step5: System adds a child node HK-Direct-rout-hinet to the node list, node_type = routed
routedOutbound.step6: Check this child node in package management and assign to specified users to take effect
routedOutbound.routingExampleText: After the admin creates HK-Direct-rout-hinet (landing HINET) and HK-Direct-rout-seednet (landing Seednet) routed outbound child nodes:
routedOutbound.emailFormatNote: Actual email format: <username>__<short>__<label>, admin placeholder is _admin__<short>__<label>. short is a randomly generated identifier when creating the routed outbound, used to distinguish multiple creations with the same label.
routedOutbound.userViewHeading: Regular User Perspective (Per-User Private Routed Outbound)
routedOutbound.userViewText: After the admin enables Allow Users to Create Routed Outbounds in System Settings, regular users can create their own exclusive exits, independent of package assignment, effective immediately. Differences from admin path: skips admin placeholder, rule.user is set directly to the user's sub-account email; node routed_owner = 'user', visible and deletable only by the creator.
routedOutbound.switchQuotaLocation: Location: Admin login -> System Settings -> MiaoMiaoWu Feature Settings card -> Allow Users to Create Routed Outbounds row.
routedOutbound.switchOff: Off (default): Regular users don't see the route button on node rows; API POST returns 403.
routedOutbound.switchOn: On: After toggling on, the Quantity and Daily Limit input fields become enabled.
routedOutbound.switchQuantity: Quantity: Maximum number of routed outbounds held. Default 2; adjustable to any integer >= 1. Unlimited is not supported (each routed outbound adds an outbound + routing rule to xray, unlimited could cause config bloat).
routedOutbound.switchDaily: Daily Limit: Total create + delete operations per day. Default 5; adjustable to any integer >= 1. Unlimited is not supported. Design reason: xray routing doesn't support gRPC dynamic loading, so agent auto-restarts xray after each routed outbound change. Frequent restarts affect other users' connections, hence rate limiting by operation count.
routedOutbound.switchWindow: Counting window: Based on master server local timezone, from 00:00 to 24:00; not rolling.
routedOutbound.switchSaveImmediate: Changes take effect immediately: Saved to system_settings table when switch is toggled or input field loses focus.
routedOutbound.userStep1: Regular user finds a visible transit node on the node management page (package-assigned or self-imported), clicks the route button
routedOutbound.userStep2: Dialog only shows Routed Outbound (Per User) mode (admin's Entire Node radio + server Tab are hidden)
routedOutbound.userStep3: Select a landing node from the Select from Existing Nodes tab
routedOutbound.userStep4: Label auto-fills as rout-<target slug>, can be manually changed
routedOutbound.userCreateResult: System adds a new row <transit node>-<label> to the node list, visible only to the creator, automatically included in user subscription.
routedOutbound.userDeleteText: The created routed outbound node row shows a delete button on the right (visible only to the creator). Clicking it cleans up the corresponding outbound, routing rule, user client on xray, as well as the node row + user_subaccounts record in DB. Deletion is not restricted by the enabled toggle -- even if the admin turns off the switch, users can still clean up existing routed outbounds.
routedOutbound.suspendTriggerIntro: The following three situations trigger automatic suspension of user private routed outbounds (removes rule + client, retains outbound + credentials):
routedOutbound.suspendTrigger3: Traffic limit exceeded (traffic limit enforcer blacklists)
routedOutbound.resumeTriggerIntro: Corresponding resume triggers (rebuilds rule + adds back client):
routedOutbound.suspendPackageExpireNote: Note: When package expires, the routed outbound is auto-suspended due to loss of access to the parent inbound, but won't auto-resume (requires rebinding a package).
routedOutbound.advancedHeading: Advanced: Multi-Landing Traffic Sharing on One Transit
routedOutbound.advancedText: Mount multiple outbounds on a single transit inbound, reusing the transit's port, UUID, and Reality config, directly assignable to users via packages. Ideal for multi-region routing with a single transit server.
routedOutbound.advancedScenarioText: One HK-Direct transit + two landings (HKT, HINET), creating two routed child nodes via routed outbound:
routedOutbound.advancedEffectText: Assign both child nodes in a package to users. Users see two routes in their subscription, sharing the same underlying transit inbound:
routedOutbound.advancedEffect1: Saves transit server ports and Reality config (no need to open separate inbounds for each landing)
routedOutbound.advancedEffect2: Traffic naturally aggregates at the transit inbound level (easy to monitor total transit pressure)
routedOutbound.advancedEffect3: User-level traffic is still billed independently by sub-account email
routedOutbound.advancedEffect4: Users seamlessly switch between landings, experiencing them as two independent routes
routedOutbound.implCreate1: 1. Add a placeholder admin client to the parent physical node's inbound (email = _admin__<short>__<label>)
routedOutbound.implCreate2: 2. Add an outbound on the source server, tag = routed:p<parent node id>:<label>
routedOutbound.implCreate3: 3. Prepend a rule with marktag to the routing rules array, user = [admin_email]
routedOutbound.implCreate4: 4. Write a child node row to the master database nodes table (node_type=routed, parent_node_id, routed_outbound_tag)
routedOutbound.implAssignHeading: When Package Assigns User to Child Node
routedOutbound.implAssign1: 1. Add a sub-account client for the user in the parent inbound (email = <username>__<short>__<label>, UUID independently generated)
routedOutbound.implAssign2: 2. Append the email to the routing rule's user[] array
routedOutbound.implAssign3: 3. Credentials written to user_subaccounts table, reused on renewal/resume
routedOutbound.implSubscriptionText: The routed outbound child node's clash_config comes from the parent inbound. During subscription generation, the UUID in the node is replaced with the user's sub-account UUID, and the node name is replaced with the child node's configured NodeName. This way, when the client connects using the sub-account email, xray matches the user-constrained routing rule and routes to the specified outbound.
routedOutbound.implTrafficText: The user.<email> metric reported by xray stats is reverse-looked up via user_subaccounts.email -> username to find the mmwx username, ultimately aggregated to the user's total traffic.
routedOutbound.implTrafficMerge: Therefore, even if a user has multiple sub-accounts (subscribed to multiple routed outbound child nodes), traffic is correctly merged to their main account.
routedOutbound.comparisonHeading: Comparison: Node Level vs User Level
routedOutbound.cmpXrayRestartUser: Yes (on first creation and each new user addition)
routedOutbound.faq1Q: Can routed outbound child nodes be deleted?
routedOutbound.faq1A: Yes. Delete from the node management page or the Routed Outbound panel at the top. When deleting, the system cleans up the placeholder client on the parent inbound, all user sub-account clients, and routing rules.
routedOutbound.faq2Q: How many routed outbounds can a single parent node create?
routedOutbound.faq2A: No hard limit. Each one is distinguished by a different Label. Common usage is creating one for each landing.
routedOutbound.faq3Q: What happens when a user is assigned to multiple routed outbound child nodes?
routedOutbound.faq3A: The user will have multiple sub-account clients on the parent inbound (one for each routed outbound), seeing multiple routes in their subscription. Xray routing matches rules in order, each sub-account email hits its own rule without interference.
routedOutbound.faq4Q: Can regular users add routing rules themselves?
routedOutbound.faq4A: Directly adding routing rules is not possible (that's an admin API). However, the admin can enable Allow Users to Create Routed Outbounds in System Settings > MiaoMiaoWu Feature Settings, after which regular users can self-create private routed outbounds on their visible nodes (subject to quota, default 2). The API path is POST /api/user/routed-outbound.
routedOutbound.faq5Q: What happens to existing user routed outbounds when the toggle is turned off?
routedOutbound.faq5A: They are retained. Configured outbounds and rules are not automatically removed, and subscription nodes continue to work. Turning off only disables creating new ones. Users can still delete existing nodes (delete API is not restricted by the enabled toggle). To fully clean up, the admin can delete the user in user management, which cascades cleanup of all their private routed outbounds.
routedOutbound.faq6Q: Why is there no 'unlimited' option for quantity?
routedOutbound.faq6A: Each user private routed outbound adds an outbound + routing rule to xray config. If unlimited were allowed, malicious users or scripts could fill up xray config in a short time, causing slow restarts or OOM. Default 2, with admin-configurable upper limit, is a ballast design.
routedOutbound.faq7A: Xray's routing module doesn't support gRPC dynamic rule loading -- any rule change (add, delete, modify user[]) requires restarting the xray process. So each time a regular user creates/deletes a routed outbound, agent auto-restarts xray on the same node, briefly interrupting other users' connections (reconnection recovers, but experience is affected). Quantity limit prevents accumulation, daily operation limit prevents frequent short-term changes -- they are complementary. Default 5/day is sufficient for normal exit node switching; excess returns 429 Too Many Requests.
routedOutbound.faq8Q: How is the daily count calculated?
routedOutbound.faq8A: A new table user_routed_outbound_actions(username, action, created_at) is created in the master database. Each successful POST/DELETE (excluding validation-rejected requests) logs a create or delete entry. Validation counts rows from the current day's 00:00 in the master server's local timezone; not a rolling 24-hour window. Resets after midnight.
routedOutbound.faq9Q: Are admin creation paths subject to these two limits?
routedOutbound.faq9A: No. /api/admin/routed-outbound is for admin management of the 'package routed outbound pool' and is not subject to user quota / daily limit constraints. The two API paths also differ internally: admin path has a placeholder admin client, user path does not (see the Entire Node vs Per User comparison above).

shareServer.title: Share Server
shareServer.overviewText1: Share Server is a PRO feature of MiaoMiaoWu X that allows one MiaoMiaoWu X master (owner) to share its managed remote servers with other MiaoMiaoWu X masters (consumers). Consumers can add inbounds and nodes on shared servers without deploying an Agent, enabling cross-master server resource sharing.
shareServer.overviewText2: Internally, it uses a Federation architecture: all remote operations from consumers are forwarded through the owner's federation interface to the Agent. The owner always remains the sole direct controller of the Agent. Communication adds ECDH end-to-end encryption on top of HTTPS, ensuring the owner cannot inspect the consumer's specific operations.
shareServer.proNote: Share Server requires a PRO license with the server_share feature enabled. Both owner and consumer must hold valid licenses.
shareServer.ownerText: The owner generates a share token for their managed server and provides the owner address + share token to the consumer.
shareServer.ownerStep1: 1. On the Service Management page, find the server to share
shareServer.ownerStep2: 2. Click the Share button (Share icon) on the server card
shareServer.ownerStep3: 3. In the popup dialog, click Generate Share Token
shareServer.ownerStep4: 4. Copy the Owner Address and Share Token, provide to consumer
shareServer.tokenOnceNote: The share token is displayed only once when generated. Copy and save it immediately. Tokens are stored as SHA256 hashes and cannot be viewed again in plaintext.
shareServer.tokenGenerateDesc: Multiple tokens can be generated for the same server, provided to different consumers
shareServer.tokenRevokeDesc: After revocation, the token immediately becomes invalid and consumers using it can no longer operate the server
shareServer.tokenViewDesc: View the token list and creation times for the server, but cannot view token plaintext
shareServer.consumerText: The consumer uses the address and token provided by the owner to join the server. After joining, they can add inbounds and nodes as if managing their own server.
shareServer.consumerStep1: 1. On the Service Management page, click the Join Shared Server button
shareServer.consumerStep2: 2. Fill in the information provided by the owner
shareServer.consumerStep3: 3. Click Join, the system validates the token
shareServer.consumerStep4: 4. After successful join, the server appears in the service management list with a purple Shared label
shareServer.paramOwnerAddrDesc: The owner's MiaoMiaoWu X master domain address, e.g., https://owner.example.com
shareServer.paramShareTokenDesc: The share token generated by the owner
shareServer.paramServerNameDesc: Leave empty to auto-use the owner's server name
shareServer.paramInboundPrefixDesc: When adding inbounds on this shared server, tags are automatically prefixed (e.g., myx-) to avoid conflicts with the owner's inbound tags. Once set, it's fixed.
shareServer.permissionsText: The consumer has clear permission boundaries for shared server operations to ensure the owner's ultimate control.
shareServer.canViewStatusDesc: View connection status, traffic usage, speed, Xray/Nginx running status
shareServer.canAddInboundDesc: Create new Xray inbound configurations on the shared server (tags auto-prefixed)
shareServer.canManageInboundDesc: Edit and delete inbounds created by yourself
shareServer.canAddNodeDesc: Create nodes based on shared server inbounds, assign to users
shareServer.canViewServiceDesc: View Xray and Nginx installation status and version info
shareServer.cantXrayControlReason: Service control is managed by the owner; consumer can only view status
shareServer.cantNginxControlReason: Same as above, Nginx is managed by the owner
shareServer.cantInstallReason: Software installation and uninstallation is owner-only
shareServer.cantEditServerReason: Server name, address, traffic limits are set by the owner
shareServer.cantReshareReason: Shared servers cannot be re-shared to third parties to prevent chain trust propagation
shareServer.cantDirectAgentReason: All operations are forwarded through the owner's federation interface; consumer does not communicate with Agent directly
shareServer.secTokenHashDesc: Share tokens are stored as SHA256 hashes in the database; even the owner cannot view token plaintext
shareServer.secECDHDesc: ECDH key exchange is layered on top of HTTPS between consumer and owner, achieving end-to-end encryption. The owner cannot inspect consumer's operation content
shareServer.secPathWhitelistDesc: Federation forwarding only allows requests under the /api/child/ path to prevent unauthorized access
shareServer.secTokenRevokeDesc: Owner can revoke tokens at any time; consumer immediately loses access after revocation
shareServer.secAutoDowngradeDesc: When the owner doesn't support encryption, the consumer automatically downgrades to HTTPS + token authentication for compatibility
shareServer.scenarioCoRentDesc: Multiple MiaoMiaoWu X admins co-rent the same server. One admin deploys Agent as the owner, others join via share tokens, each managing their own inbounds and nodes independently.
shareServer.scenarioRentalDesc: The owner has multiple servers and shares some to other admins. The owner retains ultimate server control (start/stop services, install/upgrade), while consumers gain the ability to add inbounds and nodes.
shareServer.scenarioDistributedDesc: Deploy multiple MiaoMiaoWu X masters in different regions, sharing server resource pools across masters via server sharing, unified utilization of geographically distributed servers.
shareServer.noteBothPro: Both owner and consumer must hold PRO licenses with the server_share feature.
shareServer.notePrefix: Consumers are recommended to set an inbound prefix when joining to avoid tag conflicts with the owner's inbounds. Once set, the prefix is fixed and cannot be changed later.
shareServer.noteRevokeCleanup: After the owner revokes a token, inbounds created by the consumer remain in the Xray configuration, but the consumer can no longer manage or delete them. It's recommended to notify the consumer to clean up inbounds before revocation.
shareServer.noteLatency: Consumer operation latency depends on the consumer -> owner -> Agent network path. If the network between owner and Agent is good, the additional latency is usually negligible.
